learning_objectives: Explain the three core functions of search engines: crawling, indexing, and ranking
Identify the major ranking factors that influence organic search visibility
Distinguish between white-hat, gray-hat, and black-hat SEO practices
readings: Enge et al., The Art of SEO, Ch. 1-2
Google Search Central, 'How Search Works' (official documentation)

lecture_notes.introduction: Search Engine Optimization is the practice of increasing the quantity and quality of organic traffic to a website through search engine results. Understanding how search engines discover, evaluate, and rank content is the foundation of every SEO strategy.
key_concepts: Crawling: how search engine bots discover web pages by following links
Indexing: how discovered pages are analyzed, categorized, and stored in the search engine's database
Ranking: the algorithm that determines which indexed pages appear for a given query and in what order
Major ranking factors: relevance, authority (backlinks), user experience, content quality, technical health
Google algorithm updates: Panda (content quality), Penguin (link quality), BERT/MUM (language understanding)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using Google Search Console on a demo site to see how Googlebot discovers pages, which pages are indexed, and how queries trigger impressions and clicks
setup_requirements: Google Search Console access (demo site or personal project)
Google Chrome with SEO extensions (Ahrefs SEO Toolbar, MozBar)

review.value: The number of pages Googlebot will crawl on your site within a given time period; large sites must optimize crawl budget by ensuring important pages are easily discoverable and low-value pages are excluded

Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness: Google's framework for evaluating content quality, particularly important for YMYL (Your Money or Your Life) topics like health, finance, and legal advice

learning_objectives: Conduct systematic keyword research using professional tools
Classify keywords by search intent and map them to content types
Build a keyword strategy that targets terms across the marketing funnel
readings: Enge et al., The Art of SEO, Ch. 5

lecture_notes.introduction: Keyword research is the foundation of SEO strategy. It tells you what your audience is actually searching for, how many people search for it, how competitive it is, and what type of content they expect to find. Every SEO decision flows from this research.
key_concepts: Seed keywords: broad terms that define your topic area, used as starting points for research
Long-tail keywords: specific, lower-volume phrases with higher conversion intent and lower competition
Search volume, keyword difficulty, and cost-per-click as keyword evaluation metrics
Search intent classification: informational, navigational, transactional, commercial investigation
Keyword mapping: assigning target keywords to specific pages to avoid internal competition (cannibalization)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a complete keyword research session for a fitness website: generating seed keywords, expanding with tools, filtering by intent and difficulty, and mapping keywords to a content plan

review.value: Specific, multi-word search phrases with lower search volume but higher conversion rates and lower competition; e.g., 'best running shoes for flat feet' vs. 'running shoes'
When multiple pages on the same site target the same keyword, competing with each other in search results and diluting ranking potential; avoided through deliberate keyword mapping

learning_objectives: Optimize page elements (title tags, meta descriptions, headings, URLs) for target keywords
Structure content for featured snippets and other SERP features
Apply internal linking strategy to distribute authority and guide users
readings: Enge et al., The Art of SEO, Ch. 6-7

lecture_notes.introduction: On-page SEO is everything you control on your own website that affects rankings. It is the fastest SEO lever to pull because it depends only on your own actions. A well-optimized page can rank significantly higher than a poorly optimized page with the same content quality.
key_concepts: Title tags: the single most important on-page element, 50-60 characters, keyword near the front
Meta descriptions: 155-character ad copy for search results, not a ranking factor but affects CTR
Heading hierarchy: H1 (page title, one per page), H2-H3 (section structure, keyword-informed)
URL structure: short, descriptive, hyphen-separated, keyword-inclusive
Internal linking: connecting related pages to distribute authority and create logical site architecture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Auditing and optimizing an existing blog post: rewriting the title tag, meta description, URL, and heading structure, adding internal links, and structuring content for featured snippet eligibility

review.value: The HTML title element displayed in search results and browser tabs; place the primary keyword near the beginning, keep under 60 characters, and make it compelling enough to earn clicks
A highlighted excerpt at the top of Google results (position zero) that directly answers a query; structured content with clear definitions, numbered lists, or tables is more likely to be selected

class_schedule.topic: Technical SEO: Site Health and Performance
learning_objectives: Audit a website's technical SEO health using crawling tools
Identify and resolve common technical SEO issues: site speed, mobile usability, crawl errors
Implement structured data markup to enhance search result appearance
readings: Enge et al., The Art of SEO, Ch. 10-11

lecture_notes.introduction: Technical SEO ensures that search engines can efficiently discover, crawl, and render your website. Even the best content cannot rank if the site has technical barriers that prevent search engines from accessing and understanding it.
key_concepts: Site speed: Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) as ranking signals and user experience metrics
Mobile-first indexing: Google primarily uses the mobile version of a site for indexing and ranking
Crawl errors: 404s, redirect chains, blocked resources, orphan pages
XML sitemaps: providing search engines with a map of important pages and their update frequency
Structured data (Schema.org): markup that helps search engines understand content type and display rich results
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a full technical audit on a demo site using Screaming Frog: identifying crawl errors, broken links, missing meta tags, slow pages, and missing structured data, then prioritizing fixes by impact
setup_requirements: Screaming Frog SEO Spider (free version, 500 URL limit)

review.value: Three user experience metrics: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint, loading speed), FID/INP (First Input Delay/Interaction to Next Paint, interactivity), CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift, visual stability), used by Google as ranking signals

JSON-LD markup added to web pages that explicitly tells search engines what the content represents (article, product, FAQ, event), enabling rich results like star ratings, FAQ dropdowns, and event listings in search results

learning_objectives: Explain how backlinks function as authority signals in search algorithms
Develop ethical link building strategies including content-driven and outreach approaches
Evaluate link quality using metrics like domain authority and relevance
readings: Enge et al., The Art of SEO, Ch. 8-9
Ahrefs, 'Link Building for SEO: The Definitive Guide'

lecture_notes.introduction: Backlinks remain one of the strongest ranking signals. A link from another website to yours is a vote of confidence that tells search engines your content is valuable enough for someone else to reference. Quality, relevance, and diversity of links matter far more than quantity.
key_concepts: PageRank concept: links as votes of authority that pass ranking power from one page to another
Link quality factors: domain authority, page authority, relevance, anchor text, dofollow vs. nofollow
Content-driven link building: creating linkable assets (research, tools, guides) that earn links naturally
Outreach strategies: guest posting, broken link building, resource page outreach, digital PR
Toxic links: low-quality, spammy, or paid links that can harm rankings; disavow tool as a remedy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a backlink audit using Ahrefs: analyzing a competitor's link profile, identifying their most successful linkable assets, and developing an outreach strategy to acquire similar links
setup_requirements: Ahrefs or Moz Link Explorer (free trial)

review.value: A score (1-100) predicting how likely a domain is to rank in search results, based on the quantity and quality of its backlink profile; links from high-authority domains pass more ranking power
Content specifically designed to attract backlinks: original research, comprehensive guides, free tools, infographics, or data visualizations that other sites want to reference and link to

learning_objectives: Build an SEO reporting dashboard using Google Search Console and analytics data
Interpret ranking, traffic, and conversion trends to identify optimization opportunities
Develop a continuous optimization plan with prioritized action items

lecture_notes.introduction: SEO is not a one-time project. It is a continuous optimization practice where you monitor performance, identify opportunities and problems, take action, and measure results. The ability to translate data into actionable insights is what separates SEO professionals from practitioners who follow checklists.
key_concepts: Search Console metrics: impressions, clicks, CTR, average position for queries and pages
Traffic trend analysis: identifying growth, decline, and seasonal patterns in organic traffic
Ranking opportunity identification: pages ranking on page 2 that could reach page 1 with targeted optimization
Content refresh strategy: updating existing content to maintain or improve rankings over time
SEO reporting: translating technical metrics into business-relevant insights for stakeholders
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a monthly SEO report from Search Console and Google Analytics data: traffic overview, top performing pages, ranking movements, opportunities, and recommended actions with projected impact
setup_requirements: Google Data Studio (Looker Studio) for dashboard creation

review.value: Keywords where your page ranks in positions 5-20; with targeted optimization (content improvement, internal linking, meta tag refinement), these pages have the highest probability of moving to high-traffic positions on page 1
Updating existing published content with new information, improved structure, and additional depth to maintain or recover search rankings, often more efficient than creating new content from scratch

questions.question: Explain the difference between informational and transactional search intent. For the keyword 'email marketing software,' determine the likely intent and describe what type of content Google would rank highest.
questions.answer: Informational intent means the searcher wants to learn something (e.g., 'what is email marketing'). Transactional intent means the searcher wants to take action, usually a purchase (e.g., 'buy Mailchimp'). 'Email marketing software' has commercial investigation intent (a subset of transactional): the searcher is comparing options before making a purchase decision. Google would rank comparison content highest: listicles like 'Best Email Marketing Software in 2024,' feature comparison tables, and review aggregators, because this content type best matches the intent of someone evaluating options.

rubric.key: Correctly defines informational and transactional intent
Identifies correct intent for the keyword
Describes appropriate content type with reasoning

questions.question: What is a featured snippet, and describe two content formatting techniques that increase the likelihood of earning one.
questions.answer: A featured snippet is a highlighted excerpt displayed at the top of Google's search results (position zero) that directly answers a search query. Technique 1: For definition queries, place a concise, clear definition (40-50 words) immediately after an H2 heading that matches the search query, using the pattern 'X is...' to give Google a clean extractable answer. Technique 2: For process or list queries, use numbered or bulleted lists with clear, consistent formatting, starting each item with an action verb or key term, making it easy for Google to extract the list as a snippet.

rubric.key: First formatting technique is specific and actionable
Second formatting technique is specific and actionable

questions.question: List the three Core Web Vitals metrics and explain why Google uses them as ranking signals.
questions.answer: The three Core Web Vitals are: (1) LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): measures loading speed by timing when the largest visible content element renders, target under 2.5 seconds. (2) INP (Interaction to Next Paint, replacing FID): measures interactivity by timing how quickly the page responds to user input, target under 200ms. (3) CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): measures visual stability by tracking how much visible elements shift during page loading, target under 0.1. Google uses these as ranking signals because they directly measure user experience. A slow, unresponsive, or visually unstable page frustrates users, and Google wants to rank pages that provide a good user experience alongside relevant content.

rubric.key: Lists all three metrics with correct definitions
Explains Google's rationale for using them

questions.question: What is a linkable asset? Describe one specific type and explain why it attracts backlinks more effectively than standard blog content.
questions.answer: A linkable asset is content specifically designed to attract backlinks by providing unique value that other sites want to reference. Example: an original research study or industry survey. This attracts backlinks more effectively than standard blog posts because it provides data and findings that do not exist elsewhere. When journalists, bloggers, and industry professionals need to cite statistics or trends, they link to the original source. Standard blog posts typically summarize existing information, giving other sites no reason to link since they could link to the original sources instead.

rubric.key: Explains why it attracts links better than standard content

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive SEO Audit and Strategy
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive SEO audit of a real website covering technical health, on-page optimization, content quality, and backlink profile. Deliver a prioritized strategy document with specific recommendations, projected impact, and an implementation timeline.
objectives: Perform a technical SEO audit identifying crawl, indexing, and performance issues
Evaluate on-page optimization and content quality across key pages
Analyze the backlink profile and identify link building opportunities
Deliver a prioritized action plan with projected impact and timeline
deliverables: Technical audit report covering site speed, mobile usability, crawl errors, and structured data
On-page analysis of 10 key pages with specific optimization recommendations
Backlink profile analysis with competitor comparison and link building strategy
Prioritized action plan with estimated effort, impact, and 90-day implementation timeline
Executive summary (1 page) suitable for non-technical stakeholders

criteria: Crawl analysis is thorough and identifies genuine issues
Site speed analysis includes Core Web Vitals assessment
Mobile usability issues are identified and documented
Structured data opportunities are identified
10 key pages are analyzed with specific, actionable recommendations
Keyword targeting is evaluated for each page
Content quality is assessed against E-E-A-T standards
Internal linking opportunities are identified
Backlink profile is analyzed with appropriate tools
Competitor comparison provides strategic context
Link building opportunities are specific and achievable
Toxic link risks are assessed
Recommendations are prioritized by impact and effort
Implementation timeline is realistic
Projected impact estimates are grounded in data
Executive summary communicates key findings to non-technical audience
Thorough technical audit demonstrating tool proficiency and analytical skill
Detailed on-page analysis with specific, actionable optimizations
Strategic backlink analysis with competitive insight
Professional strategy document with clear prioritization and timeline

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on SEO concepts, tools, and best practices

weekly_audits.description: Weekly hands-on SEO auditing and optimization exercises

major_assignment.description: Comprehensive SEO audit and strategy for a real website

midterm_examination.description: Practical examination covering keyword research, on-page optimization, and technical SEO

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination including live SEO analysis and strategy development

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all sessions. SEO tools are demonstrated live and hands-on exercises require in-class participation. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. SEO audit deliverables submitted more than 5 days late will not be accepted.
course_policies.collaboration: Weekly exercises may be completed in pairs. The major assignment is individual work. Sharing audit reports, keyword research, or strategy documents between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All audit data must come from stated tools and sources. Fabricating rankings, traffic data, or backlink metrics is academic fraud. Screenshots used in reports must be authentic.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Google Search Console access (personal site or demo), Chrome with SEO extensions, Screaming Frog (free version), and access to Ahrefs or SEMrush (student accounts provided). A laptop with reliable internet is required for all sessions.
